records.formula_explanation: The visible statement says that if $X_1,\ldots,X_n$ are Bernoulli$(p)$ and $X$ is their sum, then $X$ has a binomial distribution with parameters $n$ and $p$. It identifies the count of successes in $n$ Bernoulli trials.

records.nearby_text_before: Example: Profile CI Derive the profile CI for p Start with the likelihood
records.nearby_text_after: Now obtain the log-likelihood at p and at 𝑝
records.formula_explanation: The visible formula gives the likelihood for parameter p in a binomial model: p is raised to x and (1-p) to n-x. It represents the probability of observing x successes out of n trials, up to a proportionality constant if needed.

records.nearby_text_before: Derive the profile CI for p Start with the likelihood Now obtain the log-likelihood at p and at 𝑝

records.formula_explanation: This gives the log-likelihood for a binomial proportion $p$ and then the same expression evaluated at the estimate $\hat p$. It shows the observed count split into $x$ successes and $n-x$ failures.

records.nearby_text_before: Example: Profile CI

records.formula_latex: Let X_1,\ldots,X_n \sim \mathrm{Bernoulli}(p) \text{ and } X=\sum_{i=1}^n X_i. \text{ Then } X\sim \mathrm{Binom}(n,p)
records.formula_explanation: The formula states that if $X_1,\ldots,X_n$ are Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables and $X$ is their sum, then $X$ has a binomial distribution with parameters $n$ and $p$. It gives the standard relationship between repeated Bernoulli trials and a binomial count.

records.formula_explanation: This defines the set of parameter values \(\theta\) for which the log-likelihood drop from the maximum is less than the chi-square cutoff \(\chi_\alpha^2\). It is a likelihood-ratio confidence region.

records.nearby_text_before: -The average error that results from using a statistical method to predict the response on a new observation -The prediction error over an independent test sample -The average loss over the training sample

records.formula_explanation: This formula is the average loss computed over the training sample. It sums the loss between each observed response \(y_i\) and the model prediction \(\hat{f}(x_i)\), then divides by \(N\).

records.nearby_text_before: In regression settings there is a similar result that explains the tradeoff between bias and variance What is the ' true value of the parameter ' aka ideal predictor ? What is the estimator aka learned predictor ?

records.formula_explanation: This defines the fitted function \(\hat f(x)\) as the function in \(\mathcal{F}\) that minimizes the sum of squared residuals. It is the empirical least-squares training objective over the data points \((x_i,y_i)\).

records.nearby_text_before: What is the ' true value of the parameter ' aka ideal predictor ? What is the estimator aka learned predictor ? What is the MSE aka average learning error ?

records.formula_explanation: This formula decomposes the mean squared error of the learned predictor \(\hat f(x)\) from the target \(\eta(x)\) into a squared bias term plus a variance term. It shows how prediction error splits into systematic error and variability across samples.

records.nearby_text_before: What is the ' true value of the parameter ' aka ideal predictor ? What is the estimator aka learned predictor ? What is the MSE aka average learning error ?
records.formula_explanation: This is the bias-variance decomposition of mean squared error. The first term is the squared bias and the second term is the variance of the estimator around its expectation.

records.nearby_text_before: Instead of creating two subsets of comparable size, a single observation is used for the validation set and the remaining observations ( n -1) make up the training set. 123
records.nearby_text_after: ▪ LOOCV Algorithm -Split the entire data set of size n into Blue = training data set
records.formula_explanation: This formula defines CV(n) as the average of the n mean squared errors, one from each validation split. It summarizes overall cross-validation performance by averaging the individual MSE values.

records.nearby_text_before: Performing LOOCV multiple times always yields the same results because there is no randomness in the training/validation set splits LOOCV is computationally intensive because the model has to be fit n times. However, there is a shortcut with OLS linear or polynomial regression (where hi is the leverage)
records.formula_explanation: This gives the leave-one-out cross-validation estimate as the average of squared adjusted residuals. Each residual is divided by \(1-h_i\), where \(h_i\) is the leverage for observation \(i\).

records.nearby_text_before: The Bootstrap: Overview (cont.) Z*2 S ( Z ) is any quantity computed from the data Z , for example, the prediction at some input point From the bootstrap sampling we can estimate any aspect of the distribution of S ( Z ), for example, its variance

records.formula_explanation: This is the bootstrap estimate of the variance of the statistic $S(Z)$. It computes the sample variance of the bootstrap replicates $S(Z^{*b})$ around their average $\bar S^{*}$.

records.nearby_text_before: To do so, the process of simulating 100 paired observations of X and Y is repeated 1,000 times; now we have 1,000 estimates for α The mean over all 1,000 estimates for α → The standard deviation of the estimates →

records.nearby_text_before: This procedure is repeated B times for some large value of B (say 100 or 1000), in order to produce B different bootstrap data sets, 𝑍 ∗1 , 𝑍 ∗2 , … , 𝑍 ∗𝐵 , and B corresponding α estimates, ො 𝛼 ∗1 , ො 𝛼 ∗2 , … , ො 𝛼 ∗𝐵 We compute the standard error of these bootstrap estimates using

records.formula_explanation: This formula averages the loss over all observations and all bootstrap-fitted models. It gives the bootstrap estimate of prediction error, using the fitted model from each bootstrap sample on the original data points.

records.nearby_text_before: Hypothesis tests allow us to take a sample of data from a population and infer about the plausibility of competing hypotheses A hypothesis is a claim about the value of the unknown population parameter
records.nearby_text_after: There will always be two hypotheses; called null and alternative Goal: Based on the sample, decide in favor of either 𝐻0 or 𝐻𝑎
records.formula_explanation: The formula states two competing hypotheses for a test: the null hypothesis is that the population mean is at most 0, and the alternative hypothesis is that the mean is greater than 0. It is a one-sided hypothesis test setup.

records.formula_explanation: The visible formula defines Mallow’s $C_p$ as the ratio $SSE_p/MSE$, adjusted by subtracting $n$ and adding $2(p+1)$. It is a model-selection score used to compare candidate regression models.

records.nearby_text_before: The Akaike Information Criterion, or AIC for short, is a method for scoring and selecting a model. It is named for the developer of the method, Hirotugu Akaike, and may be shown to have a basis in information theory and frequentist-based inference. BIC is a similar concept The AIC and BIC are both based on -2 times the maximized value of the loglikelihood (-2 LL ); k is the number of parameters and LL is the log-likelihood value at the MLE

records.formula_explanation: The formula defines the unit deviance d(y,\mu) as twice the difference between t(y,y) and t(y,\mu). It measures how far the value at \mu is from its maximum at \mu=y, so it is zero when y=\mu.

records.nearby_text_before: Observations are independent (standard statistical assumption except in time series analysis and Markov chains) The distribution of Y (conditional on the X variables) follows a Poisson distribution
records.nearby_text_after: Recall that 𝜆 > 0 is a parameter of the distribution equal to both the mean and the variance Because of the properties of the Poisson distribution, the Poisson regression model is known to satisfy the equidispersion property. If data indicate overdispersion (variance greater than the mean), then alternatives like the negative binomial regression might be more appropriate
records.formula_explanation: The visible formula says that, given X, the variable Y follows a Poisson distribution with parameter \(\lambda X\). It expresses a conditional sampling assumption for the model.

records.nearby_text_before: Poisson Regression Model Log-Linear model
records.nearby_text_after: Like the logistic regression, exp( b j ) represents the multiplicative effect of the j th predictor on the mean, keeping the other predictors fixed. That is, increasing Xj by one unit multiplies the mean of the response variable by a factor of exp( b j ) Empirical evidence shows that with count data the effects of predictors are often multiplicative rather than additive.
